title: Captive Kashmir: Story of a Betrayed and Enslaved People
description: Captive Kashmir: Story of a Betrayed and Enslaved People is a book by Aziz Beg. This book is highly critical of India's stand on Kashmir.
descriptionParagraphs: Captive Kashmir: Story of a Betrayed and Enslaved People is a book by Aziz Beg. This book is highly critical of India's stand on Kashmir.

The surviving record is interesting because it shows how even ordinary-looking books can acquire a charged political afterlife. As a book, it can be read not only for subject matter but for the way form, tone, and circulation make a text feel dangerous, intimate, or politically usable to anxious officials.

It also matters as part of a wider censorship history in India. The present page is a dossier starter built from source-tracked ban records; the surviving note currently says: This book is highly critical of India's stand on Kashmir. This book cannot be imported into India. More publication history, translations, and close reading can be added later.

banningParagraphs: Captive Kashmir: Story of a Betrayed and Enslaved People entered censorship debates as a book associated with controversy, publication history, and state scrutiny. In the current dossier, the main state objections cluster around political sensitivity.

The earliest event currently captured here is 1958 in India, where Government of India or British Indian authorities banned publication, sale, or possession. counterReadings.title: 100 Banned Books: Censorship Histories of World Literature
counterReadings.author: Nicholas J. Karolides, Margaret Bald, and Dawn B. Sova
counterReadings.note: A compact reference on how censorship systems moved across states, churches, and courts.

counterReadings.title: Banned Books: 387 B.C. to 1978 A.D.
counterReadings.author: Anne Lyon Haight
counterReadings.note: Useful for comparing older obscenity, heresy, and political bans with modern free-speech disputes.
